meta.title: Create a computer program
meta.purpose: Students are able to create a computer program that meets given specifications and demonstrates testing, debugging, and documentation.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

key_terms.definition: Testing values just below, on, and just above a defined limit to confirm correct program behaviour.
Input outside the allowed domain that the program must handle gracefully.

omis.description: Program stores at least two distinct data types in variables.

positive_signals: Explicit assignment of integer values to variables
Explicit assignment of string values to variables
Explicit assignment of boolean or float values to variables
Clear use of at least two different variable data types
negative_signals: Numbers stored as strings without reason
Variables declared but never assigned or used
Only one data type used despite multiple variables
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Collections like lists do not count as a second data type unless the student also uses another distinct variable data type.

omis.description: Program uses sequence, selection, iteration, and accesses data in a collection.

positive_signals: Use of if/else or another conditional structure
Use of a loop for repeated processing
Collection access such as append, index access, or iteration over stored items
Logical sequence of statements showing program flow
negative_signals: Collection created but never meaningfully accessed
Missing selection logic
Missing iteration
Only sequence is visible with no collection processing
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Sequence alone is not enough; the program must also show selection, iteration, and actual collection access.

omis.source_ref: AS PDF p1 ‘The computer program must…’

omis.description: Testing evidence shows the program works with expected inputs.

positive_signals: Specific test cases with clear inputs and observed outputs
Expected outputs are shown or implied clearly enough to compare
Testing evidence appears in tables, logs, notes, screenshots, or screencasts
Results show the program working for normal expected cases
negative_signals: Generic claims such as 'it worked' without evidence
Inputs listed without any outputs
Expected and actual behaviour cannot be compared
No concrete test details are shown
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Testing requires concrete evidence of what was tried and what happened, not just a statement that the program was tested.

omis.description: Debugging evidence shows at least one defect was identified and fixed.

positive_signals: A specific bug or defect is described clearly
A code or logic change is shown or explained as the fix
Retesting shows the defect was resolved
Evidence appears in notes, before/after code, logs, screenshots, or screencasts
negative_signals: Claims that debugging happened without naming the issue
A fix is mentioned but the original problem is unclear
A bug is identified but no fix is shown
No evidence that the program was retested after the fix
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Debugging evidence must show both the problem and the change made to fix it; a corrected final program on its own is not enough.

success_examples: Bug: loop never terminated when input was 0 → Fix: changed condition from '>' to '>=' → Retest: input 0 now exits correctly

omis.description: Code contains comments documenting the program.

positive_signals: At least two comments appear in different parts of the code
Comments describe purpose or behaviour of code sections
Comments are more than single-word labels
Comments help a reader understand what the program is doing
negative_signals: Single-word comments such as '# loop'
Comments only on trivial lines
Comments clustered in one place only
Line-by-line comments that simply restate code
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Comments should help document the program for a reader, not just translate each line into plain English.

success_examples: # Ask user to guess the number

omis.description: Variable names are succinct and descriptive.

positive_signals: Variable names reflect their content or role
Names are short enough to stay readable
Names follow sensible language conventions
Names avoid vague placeholders like x1 or tmp
negative_signals: Non-descriptive names such as x, tmp, or a1
Names that do not match the variable purpose
Inconsistent naming style
Names that are confusing or unnecessarily long
evidence_guide.common_confusion: A few conventional short names may be acceptable, but the main program variables should still communicate their purpose clearly.

omis.description: Comments consistently explain the purpose of each code section.

positive_signals: Comments explain why a code section exists
Important sections are commented consistently
Comments clarify the intent of the code
Purpose-level comments appear across the program
negative_signals: Comments only describe what each line does
Major sections have no explanatory comments
Comments justify choices without clarifying purpose
Commenting is inconsistent across the program
evidence_guide.common_confusion: For Merit, comments should explain the purpose of sections, not just narrate the operations line by line.

success_examples: # Check if the car is valid

omis.description: Testing evidence includes at least one on-, below-, and above-boundary value for an identified boundary (where a boundary exists).

positive_signals: A clear boundary is identified from the task or program logic
Specific below, on, and above values are tested
Observed outputs are shown for the boundary tests
Boundary testing is documented as part of the testing evidence
negative_signals: Vague mention of boundary testing with no values
Only one side of the boundary is tested
The boundary itself is not identified
Boundary testing is claimed where no meaningful boundary exists
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Boundary testing applies only when the task or program has a real identifiable boundary; if no such boundary exists, this should be treated as not applicable rather than invented.

omis.description: Conditions and control structures are used effectively to reduce repetition and errors.

positive_signals: Chained or nested conditions reduce repeated code
Boolean operators are used meaningfully in conditions
Condition order supports clear and reliable behaviour
Loop structures naturally reduce repetition and mistakes
negative_signals: Duplicated logic across the program
Redundant or inefficient checks
Simple repetitive conditions where cleaner logic was possible
Control structures are present but do not improve reliability or efficiency
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Excellence is not about having more control structures; it is about using them in ways that reduce repetition and potential error.

success_examples: while guess != target and attempts < MAX_TRIES:

omis.description: Program uses constants, variables, or derived values instead of hard-coded literals to enable flexibility.

positive_signals: Constants are used for values likely to change
Derived calculations replace repeated literals
Values are easy to change without rewriting many lines
The code is structured for maintainability
negative_signals: Repeated hard-coded literals appear without reason
Magic numbers are embedded directly in logic
Literal values make the program harder to update
No use of constants or derived values where clearly helpful
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Not every literal is a problem, but values that may need to change or are repeated should usually be lifted into constants or derived expressions.

omis.description: Testing demonstrates program handles at least one invalid input gracefully (where user/input validation is applicable).

positive_signals: At least one clearly invalid input is tested
The program responds with an error message, retry, or safe handling path
Testing shows the program does not crash on invalid input
Invalid-input evidence is documented with outcome
negative_signals: Only valid inputs are tested
Invalid input causes a crash or traceback
Invalid cases are mentioned but not demonstrated
Invalid-input testing is claimed where there is no meaningful input surface
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Invalid-input testing is only applicable when the program actually accepts user or external input; if there is no meaningful invalid-input surface, this should be treated as not applicable rather than fabricated.
